Biography

I am an Associate Professor in the department of Computer Science and

Engineering at Texas A&M University. I have joined here in Fall 2028. My research

area is broadly computer architecture and systems with recent focus on machine

learning and security.

Catalog Description

Computer Organization. (3-2). Credit 4. Computer systems from programmer's

perspective including simple logic design, data representation and processor

architecture, programming of processors, memory, control flow, input/output, and

performance measurements; hands-on lab assignments. Prerequisite: Grade of C

or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

Additional Course Details

The goal of this course is to provide the student with a working knowledge of different

methods for logic representation, manipulation, use of hardware and logic simulation tools

to describe and simulate computer operation, instruction set architecture, and central

processing unit organization. At the end of the course the student should be able to view the

design of digital systems from a new perspective and have an understanding of several

fundamental concepts that can be applied to a wide variety of computer architectures.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

processing unit organization. At the end of the course the student should be able to view the

design of digital systems from a new perspective and have an understanding of several

fundamental concepts that can be applied to a wide variety of computer architectures.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Perform Basic Logic Design

Understand the design of a simple computer system

Understand elements that make up a CPU (Central Processing Unit)

Design software with an understanding of how it will run on computers

Participate in discussions that involve impact of hardware and software

elements on each other and the performance of the program

Take intermediate and advanced classes in computer architecture,

systems, operating systems, cloud, parallel computing

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

Computer Systems - A Programmer's Perspective

Authors:  Randal Bryant, David O'Hallaron

Edition:  3rd

This material Is: Optional

Logic and Computer Design Fundamentals

Authors:  M. Morris Mano, Charles R. Kime and Tom Martin

Edition:  5th

Grading Policy

Grading Breakdown

Item

Percentage

Exams (3 Exams)    40%

Item

Percentage

Labs

35%

Quizzes

20%

Attendance

5%

Grading Scale:

A: 90-100%

B: 80-89%

C: 70-79%

D: 60-69%

F: 0-59%

Note: in the event that the average overall grade of all students lies below 80%, there will be

a curve on the overall grade. I will not curve the individual exams and assignments. Curving

will be done by adding necessary points to make the class average 80%. For example, if the

average overall grade is 75%, I will add 5% to every student.

Exams:

There will be three exams. Final exam will be comprehensive.

Tentative Test schedule:

Exam 1 – Feb 26, 2026 (class time)

Exam 2 – April 9, 2026 (class time)

Final Exam - April 30, 2026 (3:00 PM - 5:00 PM) in class

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Assignments/Quizzes:

Rather than traditional homework, this course will rely upon a series of on-line quiz

assessments. The goal of these quizzes will be to test your knowledge of the lecture material

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Assignments/Quizzes:

Rather than traditional homework, this course will rely upon a series of on-line quiz

assessments. The goal of these quizzes will be to test your knowledge of the lecture material

and pinpoint which lectures you should go back and review on-line. There will be one quiz

for each week, this quiz must be completed prior to the next week's lecture.

Students may retake the quiz as many times as 3 times. The highest score will be recorded.

If the quiz submission is delayed, each late day is penalized by 20%. For example, if a

quiz is submitted 2 days after the due date, it will receive 60% of the actual points.

Labs:

The course will have six or seven labs (tentative). Generally, labs and/or demos must be

completed in one or two week following the lab's introduction.    Late submissions will have

20% of the points deducted for each late day (just like quizzes).

Course Schedule

Week 1: Introduction to digital logic design

Week 2, 3: Logic design basics and combinatorial circuit design

Week 4, 5: Sequential Logic

Week 6 - 11: Assembly Language Programming

Week 12 - 15: Processor Design

AI Statement

Unless otherwise specified, the use of Automated Writing Tools, including ChatGPT and

similar artificial intelligence (AI) tools, is strictly prohibited in this course, even when

properly attributed. The use of automated writing tools is considered plagiarism and will be

handled in accordance with existing policy.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Students are expected to attend and be active discussion participants during the lectures

(answering questions posed, asking questions, commenting etc.).  Students who attend less

than 80% lectures will receive a 0 for this part of their grade .

Course Specific Makeup Work Policy

There is no make up of missed assignments, labs or exams except in extremely rare cases

such as medical emergency and such. In that case,  you need to ask explicit permission of

the instructor   before the assignment/lab/exam . Without prior explicit permission, there

will not be any make up assignments/labs/exams.
